label: Imperial Palace, Tokyo
sub: Residence of the Emperor of Japan since 1869; built on the site of Edo Castle, the former seat of the Tokugawa shogunate. The Palace and its East Gardens are a major symbol of Japanese imperial continuity.

label: Kyoto Imperial Palace
sub: Imperial seat from 794 CE (Heian period) until Emperor Meiji moved the capital to Tokyo in 1869. The current palace buildings date from 1855. Kyoto served as Japan's imperial capital for over 1,000 years.

label: Tōdai-ji Temple, Nara
sub: Home of Japan's Great Buddha (Daibutsu), dedicated in 752 CE during the Nara period. The temple's main hall is the world's largest wooden structure. A UNESCO World Heritage Site representing Japan's classical Buddhist heritage.

label: Hiroshima Peace Memorial (Atomic Bomb Dome)
sub: Preserved ruins of the Hiroshima Prefectural Industrial Promotion Hall, one of the few structures left standing near the hypocenter of the August 6, 1945 atomic bombing. Now a UNESCO World Heritage Site and global symbol of nuclear disarmament.

label: Nagasaki Peace Park
sub: Located at the hypocenter of the August 9, 1945 atomic bombing ('Fat Man'), which killed approximately 70,000 people. The park houses the Nagasaki Atomic Bomb Museum and a 10-meter peace statue. Annual memorial ceremonies are held on August 9.

label: Yasukuni Shrine, Tokyo
sub: Shinto shrine founded in 1869 to honor Japan's war dead. Enshrines approximately 2.5 million individuals who died in service of Japan, including 14 Class-A WWII war criminals enshrined in 1978. High-profile political visits trigger regular diplomatic protests from China and South Korea.

label: Mount Fuji (Fujisan)
sub: Japan's highest peak at 3,776 meters; an active stratovolcano last erupting in 1707. Revered as a sacred mountain in Shinto and Buddhist traditions for over a millennium, and a UNESCO World Heritage Cultural Site since 2013. Icon of Japanese national identity.

label: Izumo Grand Shrine (Izumo Taisha), Shimane
sub: One of Japan's oldest and most sacred Shinto shrines, dedicated to Ōkuninushi no Mikoto. Ancient records (Kojiki, 712 CE) reference the shrine. The current main hall was completed in 1744. Izumo Taisha is the site of Japan's oldest mythology and deity worship traditions.

label: Fukushima Daiichi Nuclear Plant
sub: Site of the March 11, 2011 triple disaster: a magnitude 9.0 earthquake triggered a tsunami that crippled the plant's cooling systems, causing three reactor meltdowns. The worst nuclear accident since Chernobyl. Decommissioning will take 30–40 years; treated water discharge into the Pacific began in 2023.

label: Rikuzentakata (2011 Tsunami Memorial)
sub: Coastal city in Iwate Prefecture devastated by the March 11, 2011 tsunami, which reached heights of 16 meters and killed approximately 1,758 local residents out of a population of 24,000. The 'Miracle Pine' (Ikimono no Matsu) that survived the tsunami became a symbol of resilience before dying and being preserved as a memorial.

label: Okinawa Peace Memorial Park, Itoman
sub: Site of the Battle of Okinawa (April–June 1945), the bloodiest Pacific campaign: approximately 110,000 Japanese soldiers, 12,000 US troops, and 100,000+ Okinawan civilians died. The Cornerstone of Peace monument inscribes 241,000+ names of all who died. Okinawa still hosts 70% of US military facilities in Japan.

label: Iwo Jima (Iō-tō)
sub: Site of one of the Pacific War's most iconic battles (February 19 – March 26, 1945). Japanese defenders fought from an elaborate tunnel network; 6,800 US Marines and ~21,000 of 22,000 Japanese defenders died. Joe Rosenthal's flag-raising photograph at Mount Suribachi became an enduring symbol of WWII.

label: Osaka Castle
sub: Built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi between 1583–1598 as his seat of power after unifying Japan. The castle played a central role in the Sieges of Osaka (1614–1615) in which Tokugawa Ieyasu finally defeated the Toyotomi clan. The current main tower is a 1931 reconstruction housing a historical museum.

label: Himeji Castle (White Heron Castle)
sub: Japan's finest surviving example of feudal castle architecture; the main keep was completed in 1609. One of Japan's first UNESCO World Heritage Sites (1993). Often called 'White Heron Castle' for its brilliant white exterior. It survived WWII bombing raids largely intact.

label: Nikkō Tōshōgū Shrine
sub: Lavishly decorated mausoleum of Tokugawa Ieyasu, dedicated in 1617 and expanded by his grandson Iemitsu in 1634–1636. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, Nikkō Tōshōgū exemplifies the ornate Gongen-zukuri architectural style and enshrines the founder of the 265-year Edo shogunate.

label: Nagoya (Industrial Hub)
sub: Japan's fourth-largest city and industrial heartland. Home to Toyota Motor Corporation headquarters and Japan's largest export port (Port of Nagoya). Nagoya Castle, originally built in 1612, was a seat of Tokugawa power and was heavily bombed in WWII. The broader Chūkyō region generates ~10% of Japan's GDP.

label: Sapporo, Hokkaido
sub: Capital of Hokkaido, Japan's northernmost main island. Site of the 1972 Winter Olympics (Japan's first). Hokkaido was Japan's last frontier — colonized in the Meiji era, displacing indigenous Ainu people — and now contains 22% of Japan's farmland. Hosts annual Snow Festival attracting 2 million visitors.

label: Kumamoto Castle
sub: One of Japan's most impressive feudal castles, built by daimyo Kato Kiyomasa between 1601–1607. Severely damaged in the April 2016 Kumamoto earthquakes (magnitude 7.0 and 7.3), which killed 50 people and displaced 180,000. An ongoing restoration project aims for full completion by 2052.

label: Port of Yokohama
sub: Forced open to foreign trade on July 1, 1859 following Commodore Perry's 1854 Convention of Kanagawa. Yokohama became Japan's primary international trade gateway during the Meiji modernization era. Now Japan's largest port by cargo volume and home to major foreign settlement history including Chinatown.

label: Meiji Jingū Shrine, Tokyo
sub: Shinto shrine dedicated to the deified spirits of Emperor Meiji and Empress Shōken, consecrated in 1920 after the emperor's death. Set within a 70-hectare urban forest of 120,000 trees. Receives Japan's highest number of New Year's visitors — approximately 3 million annually. Symbol of the Meiji modernization era.
